speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So the next day we went home and that was eight miles more and the roads were terrible. Boy were we ever glad when we got on the old farm. We had too much stuff on that wagon. I don't know how many sacks of apples we had. All that. I think it was that fall we lost one of the horses. I don't remember now. I remember it getting shorter and shorter.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How did you lose it?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh it just got sick. Well it was a pretty old horse too I suppose had lived its life.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I think it was that winter when we had such a hard winter. We had to haul hay on a little sled. Horses couldn't make it. That was awful. Well that's too sad to talk about that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you haul it by hand?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well we had to haul it from across our meadow because it was water. It just began to break in the spring. You know how the snow is crushed on top and then underneath be water and slush and horses would fall in here and there and jumping. So we'd haul on a little sled a couple of bales or so in the morning. Early morning when there was a good crust on. We could haul it or feed the stock. That was during the first world war when my brother Alfred was away then. But he had to take over then. Of course my dad wasn't home then. He was gone.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: When did you, after you finished what school you had, did you start working at it?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Yeah we started that. After that we got into logging camps. On the Chez Meadows and then from there. Tell me some more about what it was like to cook for that. What your job was and stuff. Well it was kind of a hard job. We didn't have much to work with. That was the worst of it. The camp was new and they had old tables, bunk tables. I don't know what you call them. Long tables with benches.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: It seemed like it was so hard to cook and it seemed like it was a little old stove they had in there. It was a line but it was a small stove for the crew. And I know we had a lot of milk at home at that time so when I'd go home I'd bring a lot of sour milk so we could make sour milk. Hotcakes in the mornings we'd make hotcakes and bacon and eggs and hash brown potatoes and toast and all kinds of cereal. They had to have everything you know, the lumberjacks. But it was kind of funny too. The boss you know he was kind of... May and I we got a queen and a couple of... they were nice young fellas. They wanted to take us to the show in Bovill and so May and I we thought well that's alright we'll go to the show.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: And when we came back that night the boss was kind of behind us. He had his car we drove in. They had a pretty nice little car to drive in. I think they had some Ford or something. And this guy he had a pretty nice car driving and he'd sneak up behind. We got through the gate there was a... Oh a bub wire fence and then they had a gate there. And when we went through one of the boys closed the gate and this other guy he didn't bother about opening the gate to go through. And he went on through and tore the gate all to zigz. And the next morning he said use girls.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: You're not going to be chasing around. Or we're going to let you go. And he told my brother about it too. He said I don't want the girls to be going out with any boys. We thought well it was none of his business. We was doing our work. We wanted to go out. So about a week after that the boss got his walking shoes. It was so funny. He got canned. The superintendent came up there and canned him. He was drinking on the job. He liked his liquor pretty well. So after that, oh I guess we was there about a week or two after he got canned. Then they laid us off. The cook and me and I. They made a bigger camp out of it and then they put in me and flunkies and cooks. I can't remember what year. Anyway we was home for a while.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I guess it was the next year when the superintendent came over and he said would you girls like to go to work?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We said yes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was this T .P. Jones?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: No, T .P. Jones was out of it then. Mr. Noggle. He was the superintendent of the woods then. He came out personally. Yeah he did. He came out there with a horse and buggy.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Or did he have his car then?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I think he had the horse and buggy and then he took us to Bovill and then we took the car. I might be wrong on that. Anyway, so we said yes we'd like to go to work. Mother didn't like to have us go. So we got our clothes, what little duds we had, high top shoes and all.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We went down to Princeton, down to camp one. Got down there and they had a big fat German cook. His name was Herman.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So the next morning we went to work for this guy. We stayed with the boss's family until they got a shack fixed for us. One of those Marion shacks. Two rooms in it. Of course they had to wash it out. They steamed it out with the engine some way. They put the hose in there and got all the bugs out of it I guess. There wasn't any bugs and we moved in anyway. Big bugs.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So anyhow, the girl that was working there, maybe I shouldn't tell this. If she ever had it read before she knew it was her.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Well, I don't know if she's the only one. It doesn't matter.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Whatever you want. Anyway, we kind of took her place.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was your job? What were your jobs?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Waiting table. Peeling potatoes. Scrubbing the floors. Washing the dishes. Just about everything we had to do. Frosting the cakes for the cook. He was a great big fat cook. German. My little ones he said. Always my little ones.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So that went on. Oh, let's see.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How long did we work down there?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well, several months anyway. Anyway, this cook, he was giving away stuff to some friends. There were some other German people lived up there. And they caught him I guess. And they let him go. Well, in the meantime they got another cook that didn't want women flunkies.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So then they said, would you like to go up to Shea Meadows?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: That's where we first started. They built that camp up real big. Then they had horses and barns and bunk houses and everything there. So of course we couldn't say no. We wanted a job. And it was only about a mile and a half from our place. And that was a nice place to be. So that's where I met my husband.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He was the cook there?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: He was the cook there. He and his brother. So they said they needed a couple flunkies up there. So the straw boss, I guess you'd call him, or the assistant superintendent Walt Pierce. He met us at the train at Helmer and took us on up to the camp. And that's where we met Al and Charlie, his brother. And that was nice there because we could go home every once in a while. It was only in the evenings when we got through with our work. We'd walk home and see how everybody was. Same in the wintertime we worked there. I don't know, for a year or two.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Could you compare the way the different cooks cooked?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh yes, there is a lot of difference. Some cooks, sloppy cooks just like this one that was down at that German cook. There was a darn many flies in that place down there. At Princeton, that was camp one, and he had raisins in his cupcakes. You didn't know whether it was flies or raisins. He'd be stirring away. You know the cook usually used their hands them days because they didn't have any machines to stir with. They'd stir away there. We didn't dare to eat any cupcakes. He had nice cakes. Beautiful cakes. Fine grain, you know. All from scratch too, not like they have nowadays. Well Al was a good cook too. He was one of the best cooks. That's what he's got a name for anyway. One of the best cooks the potlatch ever had.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was his reputation for anything, you know, was there anything particularly special about your husband's cooking?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well his cooking was more like home cooking. I mean it didn't, you know, it was, well I don't know, his food was good and clean. He always had, whenever he prepared was always good. He could take a tough piece of steak and he could make a tender steak out of it. You know, he cut his own meat and everything. And some of those cooks didn't care how they cut it. But old Hermann Beyers, he was a pretty good cook all right, but he was a sloppy cook. You know, some are sloppy. They're kind of dirty cooks.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Where's their food?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I mean he was awful fussy. Anybody talking in the dining room, he had a gun up on top of the shelf where he had his dishes. And anybody gets smart I guess. I don't know if he'd use it or not, but he kept it up there anyway. Revolver. Well I didn't tell you about, should tell you about when we were working down at Princeton why we'd go out, go to dances you know. And we'd sneak out because the cook, he didn't want us, he was another one that didn't want us to go out at all. No sir. So the bull cook, you know the bull cook, he is the guy that comes and builds fires, carries the wood and all that water. He'd come in to our, well we call it the shack. He usually comes in there in the morning early in the bill a roaring fire. You could see the fire come clear out of the chimney. Anyway, this particular, I think it was Saturday night, we went to the dance down at Kennedy Ford. That's pretty close to Cluse. And we got home about, oh I imagine about three o 'clock in the morning.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: And so me and I come into work and the bull cook says, well girls, he says, did you have a good time last night?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: The cook heard it.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Was he mad.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: He says, what are you little ones? He says, were you, did you go up to dance last night?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We said yes. And he wouldn't talk to us. It was potent. So he was mad and he had his bunk right alongside our dining room there. So when he took his afternoon off, when May and I finished up, we thought well little guy we're going to have some fun. So we set the alarm clock and put it by his door and went over to our shack and let it ring. Kind of mean. I mean he was mad he wouldn't even talk to us. Made him madder yet. He thought he owned me I guess.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Is that why some of the cooks didn't want women flunkies because they figured they'd fool around with the men?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I think so or the cooks would get jealous and they wouldn't, you know. Most of them. Well that's quite a few didn't want women cooks or women flunkies. There was another guy up, his name was Bert Clark.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Some of them wanted women flunkies because they said the women was cleaner.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: But some didn't. Of course Albert, he'd rather have women flunkies because he said they'd keep things up much better than the men.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Scrubbing floors and all your dishes and all that.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How often did you scrub the floors?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We scrubbed once a week with a broom. Pour the water on it. Brush it out the door. Just as white as could be. Use a lot of soap and maybe some lye in it too sometimes.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What was the most important thing you did to Albert?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Albert wanted me to cut hot bread. He always liked to serve hot bread out of the oven.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: You know how hard that is to cut?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Of course he always had good sharp knives. And I said I'm not going to cut that bread. It's too darn hard. We got mad and he'd come over there and he'd just slice that bread. Boy we had hot bread on the table.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: That's before I married of course. That was a different song then. I got to be a boss.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did you keep working in the camps after you married?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Yeah I worked for a while. Of course about a year and two months or three months. Biddy was born. I worked off and on to relieve second cooks or something like that.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: The logs that they made, those bunk houses and kitchen and all that. And then they just had men cooks and flunkies. I think my brother flunkied for a while. I can't remember if it was there or another camp but anyway I believe it was Team Moose Creek. They used to have separate tables for the Italians because they shipped men from Italy. And they said they didn't know how to eat with their...in the butter and all that with their fingers. I guess they didn't use their knives. I don't know how to eat or something. They had a bunch of those.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: I don't know of other nationality but they had them separate tables for them.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: They used to talk about going...what the heck did they call that?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh I can't think of it. Snipe hunting. At night.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: That's silly. They'd take a guy out in the woods. That's their fun you know they'd have in the logging camps years ago. They'd say well we gotta go snipe hunting. You take the lantern and hold a gunny sack. And then we'll go around and chase the snipe into the sack see.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What's a snipe?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: It's just supposed to be a bird I guess.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So the guy'd be standing there all night holding the lantern and holding the sack. And the guys go home and go to bed.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: That's snipe hunting.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Who would fall for that?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: A whole lot of those green horns would come to the camps. Just like we used to say in the logging camp. Albert used to use that an awful lot. Some new girl would come and go to work.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: He said will you bring me the vanilla bar? And they'd wonder what in the world is in the vanilla bar?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well he says in there you bring me the vanilla bar and he began to laugh because there's nothing to it. He's just trying to have some fun.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh yeah. Crazy things.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: But when we lived up at the Elk River and the logging camps up there why that's when Betty was about four years old. Anyway, we had two flunkies there. One girl, she was real cute. Paulson girls. One of them lives in Lewiston now, Dorothy. But anyway, they were always fooling with Albert. He'd put in Pepper Donnie's neck or something. You know all the scuffling. Always the scuffling around. So this Mildred, she walks up behind him and takes the salt, pepper, shake it down his neck. So he had some eggs. He was working there making a cake or something. He took an egg and he said he just put it on her head and just rubbed it in real good. Oh she was so mad she had to go wash her hair. And it was dinner time just practically you know. She had to go wash her hair. She wouldn't talk to him for several days. The whole egg.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Can you imagine what that would be?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: What a mess.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh we used to have so much fun in camp though.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: One time the boss, they had a little creek running across there from the warehouse to the kitchen. Little bridge there you know. Here come the boss and I was standing there and he come up and he talked to me you know and I had my back. No railing or nothing you know. Standing right here and the creek was down here. He politely gave me a push and I went in there and boy I was all wet. So I reached up and I got him by the legs and I pulled him clear into that water. I'm telling you he got wet. I thought well if I get wet you're gonna get wet too. Oh he says my watch, my watch. I don't care about your watch.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: How about me getting all wet?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh that was fun. We'd have water fights and this kid that used to be a clerk there. Call him kid. He's about 63 now I guess. He seemed like kid to me. Anyway he was a clerk there. He'd always teased Betty you know. Betty was little and she said I'll tell my dad on you. I'll tell my dad if you don't quite tease me. So we got to scuffling around there and May and I we got a hold of him to our shirt clear off. Threw him into the water. He's kind of a little guy.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Quite a character.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you get to know the Lumberjacks?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh yeah.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We're all nice guys. I was talking about the Lumberjacks being so rough but they're not. They were, they respected a person. They respected women. They'd all fight for us boy. Anybody sending in mating remarks about us boy.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Said one time they said gee she's getting so darn fat she's got two ax handles brought. Well I guess one guy he said now listen you're not going to talk about the girls like that. Ready for a fight.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Now May and I thought we were reducing instead of us gaining. Said it was Albert and his brothers trick too. We said well we got to go on a diet. We're getting too fat. So they had one of those big scales you know you stand on like you have for hay and all that and move them livers. Well every morning Charlie drill out a little bit there will make it look like we losing weight and we got the right weight on there. Boy I tell you we gained. That's why we were so broad I guess. That was fun.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Yeah we had quite a time.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Betty used to fight for her dad. The girls pick on him. Boy she'd pick up a stick or anything. She'd hit him. She'd end there to fight you know scuffle.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: They started scuffling with him boy she was right there. That little kid I can just see her yet how she walking along there you know she'd you leave my daddy alone.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: She says she can still remember that. The girls would be scuffling around and they'd pick on him you know.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Was it largely the cooks and the clerks and the people that you know sort of were centered in the camps that would get to know each other?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh yeah yeah the most it was the boss and the cooks and the flunkies and clerks. And the barn boss usually come in and have coffee and cookies it'd be eating all day there if you had it. They'd come in have coffee breaks every once in a while. Coffee is on the stove all the time. Coffee pot.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Did those meals have to be ready right on time?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Right on time. Six o 'clock in the morning. We rang the bell at five thirty everybody's up. And then you ring the second bell and that was breakfast. And they get in there and eat. And they eat about some of them eat about five minutes I think. Up and gone. Gee I don't know how they ever put the food away. That fast in a way. I've heard that from other people. I don't understand.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How come they ate so fast? Were they pressured to eat faster?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: No they didn't go to work until seven o 'clock. But they did. Well I guess they're always in a hurry. Some of them were giplers too you know. So the sooner they got out there the quicker more money they made. That's by whatever they worked at. Lumber. I mean logs.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: And out in the woods they go. Start cutting trees of course.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: But uh.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Would you fix, would all the men make it back in for dinner at noon?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: No they took lunch. They did put up lunch for them for a while but then they let them put up their own. Because some of them wanted this and some wanted that. Some wanted three sandwiches and some wanted two sandwiches. And they didn't want cheese and some wanted that. So they said let them put them up themselves. And they had a long table with all kinds of food on it. Cookies, donuts, cakes, pies. All that was on there and they could take what they want. They even had salt pork. Albert Boyle salt pork. A lot of them old guys make sandwiches out of salt pork. Especially the Swedish people. They like it. And uh. They have ham and beef and pork. Salt pork. If there was any bacon left for breakfast they'd stick that on the table. Keep coffee on there and let them fix their own thermos bottles. Then they always took so much stuff and then they'd feed the bears or the animals outside of the. Boy there's a lot of waste. Terrible.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: And eat. Then they'd come in starved at night. Lumberjacks come a -plawn in there.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Then the girls would be filling up the dishes. Filling up the dishes. Go in and look it over and they'd see an empty dish come back and they'd fill her up.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: You served them? And they didn't just have big plates?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Big plates. Just family. It's family style. But you had to see that everything was filled. You know like potatoes and gravy and meat. In the mornings it'd be hotcakes. You'd be running with the hotcake plates.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: So they'd have a big hot meal and you'd have that?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh yes. They'd have steak. Usually have steak. Pork chops. Hamburger steaks.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Liver. Fish. Fridays usually. Cut up I don't know how many fish. He'd have to cut his own fish. Come in with big hunks. You know with their whole fish.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: What kind of stove would they have?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well they had big Lang. They had three ovens. Three oven stoves. Albert had bread going in the oven by the time they had breakfast going too. Or pies coming out or going in. They tried to get all their baking done in the morning besides getting up to dinner. Maybe they wouldn't have too big a crew coming in for dinner. Like you know the ones as close in. They had a lot of bread. Buns. Donuts.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: How often would that be?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Every day.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Every day?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Every day. Raised donuts. Bismarck's. Maple bars. Gosh I used to fix the maples. You know they'd put frosting on. And they'd bake cakes. Sheets. They used to make the cakes in sheets. Unless they had a smaller crew then they'd make layer cakes. White cakes. Chocolate cakes. Pies. Berry pies. Apples. Custards. Pumpkin.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Roast great big pans of meat. In the morning they'd have boiled eggs. Some mornings boiled eggs. Scrabble eggs. Fried eggs.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: All of them?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Well each morning in different mornings they'd have hotcakes. They always had hotcakes. And toast. Hash brown potatoes. That's for breakfast. Cereal. Hot cereal. Besides the cold cereal.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So that's quite a deal.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: We had fun on camp. I don't know. I just like that life up there. In the woods. I love the woods. We were a huckleberry in here. Sunday I was. It was so nice and peaceful up there. And you could sit there and pick berries and relax.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: You like pick berries?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Yeah we took one day last week.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Where'd you go?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Up by Bald Mountain.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Oh did you get some good ones?

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We got plenty for our needs.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: We made 15 pints of jam.

speaker: Laura Schrager
words: Oh did ya?

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: Yeah I sold quite a few berries. I'm going to Switzer Friday evening. A friend of mine that has condominium up there and we're going up there and stay till Sunday.

speaker: Lena (Molly) Erickson Justice
words: So where was my phone?
